objectives: Sign in with the provided demo credentials and explore the platform's features.
Find and exploit a stored XSS vulnerability to steal the admin bot's session cookie.
Use the stolen admin session to access the restricted Royalty Reports page.
Exploit a SQL injection vulnerability in the royalty search to extract the hidden flag.
hints: Track reviews accept user input and render it on the page. Try posting a review with HTML tags — does the platform sanitize them?

The admin bot at /bot/visit reads all reviews with the admin's session cookie. If your review contains JavaScript that exfiltrates document.cookie to /webhook/collect, the bot will trigger it. Check /webhook for results.
Once you have the admin's sw_session cookie, set it in your browser (document.cookie = 'sw_session=...') and navigate to /admin/royalties.

The royalty search builds its SQL query by directly embedding your input. Try injecting a single quote to cause an error, then craft a UNION SELECT with 7 columns to extract data from the platform_secrets table (columns: id, key_name, key_value).

title: SoundStage — Hidden Field Tampering
description: SoundStage is a music streaming platform where listeners curate playlists and artists track their royalties. The account settings page lets users update their profile — but does it properly verify who's being updated? Dig into the page source and see if you can access data that doesn't belong to you.

Identify the hidden form field that controls which user's profile is being updated.
Modify the hidden field to target a different user and submit the form to reveal their private data.

The hidden field named 'userId' determines whose profile gets updated. Your account might be ID 2, 3, or 4 — but what about ID 1?
Change the hidden userId field's value to 1 and submit the form. The server will process the update for that user and display their full profile.
User ID 1 belongs to the artist Luna Vega. Her profile contains an 'Artist Royalty Payment Token' field — that's where the flag is hidden.

title: SonicWave — Server-Side Request Forgery
description: SonicWave is a music streaming platform with playlists, artist dashboards, and royalty reports. The Link Preview feature on the /share page fetches any URL you provide from the server side. What URLs could the server reach that you can't?

Submit http://localhost:3000/internal/secret to the Link Preview generator. The server will fetch it internally, bypassing the browser-header check, and return the secret response containing the flag.

title: SoundSphere — XSS Session Hijack Chain
description: SoundSphere is a music streaming platform with playlists, track reviews, and an admin royalty dashboard. Trigger the admin bot to visit the reviews page, then check your webhook for the stolen session token.

hints: Check the 'My Webhooks' page — you have a personal webhook endpoint at /hooks/<your-user-id> that captures any query parameters sent to it.
Submit a track review and inspect the HTML source. Notice that review content is injected directly into the page without escaping — this is a stored XSS vulnerability.
Try a payload like: — this will execute when anyone views the page.
After posting the XSS payload, click 'Trigger Admin Bot' to make the admin bot visit the reviews page. Then check /my/hooks for the captured ss_session cookie.
Open your browser's developer console and set the stolen cookie: document.cookie = 'ss_session=<stolen_token>'; — then navigate to /admin/royalties to find the flag.

title: HarmonyStream — Stored XSS via Track Reviews
description: HarmonyStream is a music streaming platform where users can browse tracks, create playlists, and leave reviews. Trigger the admin moderation bot to visit the page and capture the admin's session data containing the flag.
hints: Navigate to any track detail page (e.g., /track/1) and try posting a review. Notice that HTML tags are rendered as-is.

title: GlobalPulse — JWT Algorithm Confusion
description: GlobalPulse is a news organization's internal journalist portal for managing articles, drafts, and editorial workflows. The platform uses JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for authentication. However, the token verification logic has a critical flaw in how it handles algorithm selection. Can you exploit the JWT implementation to gain Editor-in-Chief access and retrieve the editorial system key?
objectives: Sign in with the provided journalist credentials and explore the platform.
Examine your JWT — the Profile & Token page displays it decoded for you.
Identify the vulnerability in the JWT verification by manipulating the token's algorithm field.
Forge a token that grants you Editor-in-Chief privileges and access the restricted admin panel.
hints: Visit the Profile & Token page — your full JWT is displayed along with decoded header and payload sections. Notice the 'alg' field in the header and the 'role' field in the payload.
The JWT has three parts separated by dots: header.payload.signature. Each of the first two parts is base64url-encoded JSON. Try decoding and modifying them.
What happens if you change the 'alg' field in the header from 'HS256' to 'none'? The 'none' algorithm means no signature is required.

Forge a token: base64url-encode a header with {"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}, base64url-encode a payload with role set to 'editor-in-chief', join them with a dot and add a trailing dot (empty signature). Set this as the gp_token cookie: document.cookie = 'gp_token=' + forgedToken + '; path=/'; then navigate to /admin.

title: DailyPulse — Mass Assignment Privilege Escalation
description: DailyPulse is a newsroom management platform where journalists publish articles, manage drafts, and moderate reader comments. The Editorial Vault contains classified documents restricted to admin users. Your account has standard permissions — but the registration process might accept more than it should. Can you escalate your privileges and access the vault?
objectives: Create an account on the platform and explore the newsroom features.
Notice that your account has the 'user' role and cannot access the Editorial Vault.
Investigate how the registration endpoint processes your data — does it filter which fields are accepted?
Find a way to register with admin privileges and access the classified Editorial Vault to retrieve the flag.
hints: After registering, check your profile page. Notice your role is 'user'. The Editorial Vault page tells you it requires the 'admin' role.
The registration form only sends name, username, and password — but does the API endpoint actually restrict itself to just those fields?
Try sending the registration request manually using fetch() in the browser console or a tool like curl. Add an extra field 'role' with value 'admin' to the JSON body.

In the browser console: fetch('/api/register', { method: 'POST', headers: {'Content-Type':'application/json'}, body: JSON.stringify({name:'Hacker',username:'newadmin',password:'pass1234',role:'admin'}) }).then(r=>r.json()).then(console.log) — then log in with those credentials.

title: PulseWire — JWT Forgery & Draft Exfiltration Chain
description: PulseWire is an investigative journalism platform's internal newsroom portal. Journalists can browse published articles, view team profiles, and check their session tokens. But something about the authentication system feels off, and the editorial draft management API might be hiding more than it should. Chain two vulnerabilities together to access a confidential embargoed investigation and capture the flag.
objectives: Log in as a journalist and explore the newsroom portal. Pay close attention to how authentication works.
Examine your session token and figure out how to escalate your role to unlock the editorial features.
Access the Draft Manager and discover that the API doesn't properly restrict which drafts you can view.
Find and exfiltrate the embargoed investigation draft containing the flag.
hints: Check the /session page — it shows your full JWT token and its decoded payload. Notice the algorithm (HS256) and the 'role' field.
HS256 JWTs are signed with a symmetric secret. If the secret is a common dictionary word, tools like hashcat or jwt_tool can crack it. Try common passwords.
Once you know the secret, forge a new JWT with your role changed to 'editor'. Set it as the pw_token cookie to unlock the Draft Manager and API docs.

The Draft Manager UI only shows drafts for YOUR user ID, but the API endpoint /api/editor/drafts/:id accepts any draft ID. Try IDs starting from 1 — there's a hidden founding editor whose embargoed drafts contain sensitive information.

title: PressRoom — Break the Byline
description: PressRoom is an investigative journalism portal used by a small editorial team. Reader comments are displayed under breaking stories, the chief editor reviews them daily, and an internal API manages sensitive staff records. A classified archive record is buried deep in the system — recoverable only by someone who can chain two vulnerabilities together. Can you infiltrate the editorial suite and surface what was meant to stay hidden?
objectives: Identify and exploit a stored XSS vulnerability in the reader comment section to steal the admin's session cookie.
Trigger the editorial bot at /bot/visit and retrieve the exfiltrated session token from the webhook collector.
Impersonate the admin and enumerate a hidden user ID via the admin API's IDOR vulnerability.
Retrieve the classified private notes from the hidden system account to capture the flag.
hints: The comment form on article #1 accepts HTML. Try submitting a payload that makes an image load from a URL you control — like /webhook/collect.
After posting your XSS payload, visit /bot/visit to trigger the admin bot. Then check /webhook to see what the bot's browser sent.
With the admin session cookie in hand, set it in your browser (document.cookie = 'np_session=TOKEN') and access the admin panel or API directly.
The admin dashboard lists users starting from ID 2, but the /api/admin/users/:id endpoint will respond to any valid integer. Try enumerating lower IDs.

title: The Wire — Second-Order SQL Injection
description: The Wire is an online news platform's internal editorial portal where journalists manage articles, drafts, and comments. Registration and login are properly secured with parameterized queries, but a subtle vulnerability lurks deeper: user-supplied data stored safely during registration is later used unsafely in a different SQL query. Can you exploit this second-order SQL injection to access confidential editorial content?
objectives: Create an account on the portal and explore the dashboard, articles, and newsroom features.
Notice how the dashboard loads articles belonging to the logged-in user using their stored username.
Register with a specially crafted username that will cause SQL injection when later interpolated into a query.
Access the Editor-in-Chief's confidential draft articles that contain the flag.
hints: Registration and login use parameterized queries — direct SQL injection at those endpoints won't work. The vulnerability is second-order: data is stored safely but used unsafely later.

When you visit the dashboard or 'My Articles' page, the app builds a SQL query using your stored username to fetch your articles. If your username contains SQL metacharacters, the query structure changes.

The dashboard query looks something like: SELECT * FROM articles WHERE author_username = '<your_username>' AND status = 'published'. What happens if your username ends the string early and comments out the rest?

Register with the username admin'-- (literally: admin followed by a single quote, then two dashes). When the dashboard query interpolates this, it becomes: WHERE author_username = 'admin'--' AND status = 'published'. The -- comments out the status filter, revealing all of admin's articles including confidential drafts containing the flag.

title: La Brasería — Privilege Escalation Chain
description: La Brasería is a Michelin-starred restaurant's internal staff portal for managing menus, reservations, orders, and kitchen operations. No single vulnerability is critical — but chaining several low-severity issues together could give you access to the chef's confidential kitchen vault. Can you escalate your way from a new hire to the Head Chef's secrets?
objectives: Create a staff account on the portal and explore the available features.
Find information about other staff members that you shouldn't have access to.
Discover a credential that belongs to a privileged user.
Access the restricted kitchen vault and retrieve the confidential flag.
hints: The Staff page only lets you view your own profile in detail, but the underlying API might be more permissive. Check the API Docs page.
Try fetching /api/staff/1, /api/staff/2, etc. The Head Chef's profile has something extra that other profiles don't.
The API documentation mentions a kitchen vault endpoint that requires an API key in the X-Api-Key header.
Fetch /api/staff/1 to get the Head Chef's apiKey field, then use the browser console: fetch('/api/admin/kitchen-vault', {headers:{'X-Api-Key':'<key>'}}).then(r=>r.json()).then(console.log)

title: Casa Bellini — Kitchen Secrets
description: Casa Bellini is a fine Italian restaurant with an online ordering portal. Customers can browse the menu, view reservations, and track orders. Behind the scenes, the kitchen staff has a separate dashboard for managing the order queue. Something valuable is hidden in the restaurant's database — but accessing it requires both cunning and persistence. No single vulnerability will get you there. You'll need to chain two different exploits to uncover what the kitchen is really hiding.
objectives: Sign in with a demo account and explore the portal. Pay close attention to how your session works.
Escalate your access to reach the staff-only Kitchen dashboard.
Discover and exploit a data extraction vulnerability in the Kitchen's order lookup feature.
Extract the flag character by character from the database.
hints: Visit the Session Info page. Notice your JWT token uses HS256 signing. The HMAC secret might be a very common word — try cracking it with tools like jwt_tool, hashcat, or john.
Once you know the JWT secret, forge a new token with the 'chef' role in the payload. Set it as your bellini_session cookie to unlock the Kitchen dashboard.
The Kitchen order lookup searches by order reference (e.g., ORD-2401). The query parameter is not sanitized — try classic SQL injection patterns to confirm.

Use blind boolean SQLi with the SUBSTR oracle: ORD-2401' AND SUBSTR((SELECT secret FROM secrets LIMIT 1),1,1)='T' -- If the response shows 'Order Found', the character matches. Iterate through each position and character to extract the full flag. The API endpoint /api/kitchen/search?q=... returns JSON for easier scripting.

title: Ferro & Flame — Blind Boolean SQL Injection
description: Ferro & Flame is an upscale restaurant's internal staff portal for managing menus, reservations, orders, and kitchen operations. The menu browsing feature has a category filter that talks directly to the database — but the results never show raw query data. Something about the way it responds to different inputs feels… exploitable. Can you extract the secret stored in the database one character at a time?
objectives: Log in to the staff portal and explore the menu browsing feature.
Discover that the category filter parameter is vulnerable to SQL injection.
Confirm blind boolean behavior: the page shows 'items found' vs 'no items found' based on injected conditions.
Identify that a 'flags' table exists in the database with a 'secret' column.
Extract the full flag character by character using blind boolean-based SQL injection.
hints: The /menu page has a category filter. Try visiting /menu?category=Appetizers — items appear. Now try /menu?category=Appetizers' AND 1=1 -- (items appear) vs /menu?category=Appetizers' AND 1=0 -- (no items). The page responds differently based on truth.
There's also a JSON API at /api/menu?category= that returns {"found": true/false} — much easier to script against than parsing HTML.
To discover the flag table, try: Appetizers' AND (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM flags)>0 -- — if items appear, the 'flags' table exists.

Extract the flag character by character: Appetizers' AND SUBSTRING((SELECT secret FROM flags LIMIT 1),1,1)='T' -- . If items appear, the first character is 'T'. Iterate through all positions (the flag is 40 chars: TARANTU{<32 hex chars>}).

Write a script using fetch() in the browser console or a tool like Python requests. Loop through positions 1-40 and for each position test characters from the set: T,A,R,N,U,{,},a-f,0-9. Check if the response JSON has found:true to confirm each character.

title: Ember & Oak — Error-Based SQL Injection
description: Ember & Oak is a high-end restaurant's internal management portal used by staff to browse the menu, manage reservations, and handle kitchen orders. The application uses a SQL database to store everything — including something the chef wants kept secret. The menu filtering feature seems to have some issues with how it handles user input, and when things go wrong, the error messages are surprisingly detailed...
objectives: Log into the staff portal and explore the menu browsing feature.
Discover how the menu category filter constructs its database query.
Cause SQL errors that reveal the database schema — table names and column names.
Identify a hidden table that is not used anywhere in the normal application.
Extract the flag from the hidden table using SQL injection.
hints: The menu page has a category filter — try typing a single quote (') in the search box and clicking Filter. Watch what happens.
The error message shows the full SQL query and the SQLite error. Notice the query uses string concatenation with your input directly.

Use a UNION SELECT to query sqlite_master: try the category value: ' UNION SELECT 1,name,sql,4,5,6 FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'-- This will reveal all table schemas, including a suspicious one not visible in the app.
Once you find the hidden table (chef_special_flags), extract its contents: ' UNION SELECT 1,label,value,4,5,6 FROM chef_special_flags-- The flag is in the value column.

title: Tavola Cucina — Server-Side Request Forgery
description: Tavola Cucina is an upscale Italian restaurant's internal staff portal. The operations team recently built a 'Supplier Webhook Tester' tool that lets staff verify external API connectivity — it accepts a URL and the server fetches it on their behalf. A hidden internal configuration endpoint exists that is protected from direct browser access, but it happily responds to requests originating from the server itself. Can you abuse the webhook tester to reach the internal network?
objectives: Log in to the staff portal using one of the provided demo credentials.
Explore the portal and locate the feature that makes server-side HTTP requests to user-supplied URLs.
Identify that a protected internal endpoint exists which rejects direct browser requests.
Abuse the server-side fetch feature to make the server request its own internal endpoint and retrieve the flag.
hints: The 'Supplier Tools' section contains a webhook tester. Try entering different URLs and observe how it works — the server fetches the URL and returns the response body directly.
The server has an internal API that is not linked anywhere in the UI. Internal configuration endpoints often live at paths like /internal/, /admin/, or /config/.
When you visit /internal/secret directly in your browser, what happens? Now think about what would happen if the server fetched that same URL itself.

The server-side fetcher doesn't filter localhost URLs. Try submitting: http://localhost:3000/internal/secret — the server will fetch it without Origin or Referer headers, bypassing the access control.

title: SphereNet — Cookie-Based Role Bypass
description: SphereNet is a social networking platform where users can share posts, send messages, and connect with friends. The Admin Panel is restricted to administrators — but something about how the app handles user roles seems a little too trusting. Can you gain admin access and retrieve the platform's master key?
objectives: Log in to SphereNet using the provided demo credentials.
Explore the app and find the Admin Panel (note: it shows 'Access Denied').
Investigate how the app determines your role — check the Settings page for clues.
Modify the appropriate cookie to elevate your privileges and access the Admin Panel to retrieve the flag.
hints: Visit the Settings page — it shows your current session details, including your account role and where it comes from.
The Settings page reveals that your role is stored in a browser cookie called 'role' with the value 'user'.
Open your browser's Developer Tools (F12) → Application/Storage → Cookies. Find the 'role' cookie.
Change the 'role' cookie value from 'user' to 'admin', then navigate to /admin to access the Admin Panel and retrieve the flag.

title: PulseNet — JWT Forgery & IDOR Chain
description: PulseNet is a social media platform for creators. Beneath its polished interface, two vulnerabilities can be chained together to breach the platform founder's private messages. First, crack the authentication layer. Then, abuse a moderation tool that trusts its users a little too much. The flag is hidden in a private conversation that was never meant to be seen.
objectives: Create an account or log in with a demo account and explore the platform.
Examine how authentication works by inspecting your session token.
Elevate your privileges to access restricted platform features.
Use the moderation tools to discover and extract the flag from a hidden user's private data.
hints: Visit the Session page to see your JWT token in full. Notice it uses HS256 — a symmetric signing algorithm. What if the secret is a common word?
Try cracking the JWT secret offline using a tool like hashcat (mode 16500) or jwt_tool with a wordlist. Common passwords like 'secret', 'password', 'admin', or 'key' are worth trying first.
Once you know the signing secret, forge a new JWT with your role changed to 'moderator'. Set it as the pulse_token cookie to access the Mod Panel.
The Mod Panel has a User Lookup API at /api/mod/users/:id. The reports only show certain users — but what about user ID 1? Try looking up IDs that aren't listed in the interface.

title: Pulse — Social Network Exploit Chain
description: Pulse is a modern social network where users share posts and connect with others. Beneath its polished exterior, a chain of two critical vulnerabilities could allow an attacker to escalate from a regular user all the way to extracting top-secret platform keys. No single vulnerability gives you the flag — you must chain them together.
objectives: Create an account on Pulse and explore the social features — feed, profiles, members.
Discover that user-generated post content is rendered without sanitization.
Exploit stored XSS to steal the admin bot's session cookie and gain admin access.
Use the admin moderation panel's search feature to perform SQL injection and extract the flag from a hidden database table.
hints: Post content on the feed is rendered as raw HTML. Try posting something like bold and see if it renders.
There's a webhook collector at /webhook/collect?data=... and a viewer at /webhook. You can use these to capture exfiltrated data.
Craft an XSS payload in a post that sends document.cookie to the webhook collector, then trigger the admin bot at /bot/visit. Check /webhook for the admin's session cookie.

After stealing the admin cookie, set it in your browser (document.cookie = 'nx_session=STOLEN_VALUE') and visit /admin/moderation. The search input is vulnerable to SQL injection — try a UNION SELECT to query the platform_secrets table.
The moderation query returns 5 columns. Use: ' AND 1=0 UNION SELECT 1, key_value, key_name, classification, '' FROM platform_secrets -- to extract the flag.

title: NexusLink — SQL Injection in Search
description: NexusLink is a social networking platform where users share posts, connect with friends, and chat. The search feature lets you find posts by keyword — but the way it builds its database query might let you see more than you should. Can you uncover the hidden system note?
objectives: Log in with one of the demo accounts and explore the platform.
Use the Search Posts feature and observe how results are filtered.
Craft a search input that manipulates the underlying SQL query to bypass the visibility filter.
Retrieve the hidden flag from a post that should not be publicly visible.
hints: The search feature filters posts using a SQL LIKE clause combined with a visibility check. Only 'public' posts should appear.
Try entering a single quote (') in the search box. If you get an error, the input is being inserted directly into the SQL query.
The query likely looks like: SELECT * FROM posts WHERE content LIKE '%YOUR_INPUT%' AND visibility = 'public'. Think about how to break out of the LIKE pattern.

Use the payload: %' OR 1=1 --  (with a space after --). This closes the LIKE clause, adds a condition that's always true, and comments out the visibility check, returning ALL posts including hidden ones.

title: SkyWay — Server-Side Request Forgery to SQL Injection
description: SkyWay is a premium travel booking platform with flight search, hotel reservations, loyalty rewards, and a partner rate comparison tool. The platform includes an internal microservice architecture — but a chained vulnerability path exists that can compromise the entire system. No single flaw is critical on its own, but together they reveal secrets that should never be exposed.
objectives: Create an account and explore the SkyWay platform to understand its features and architecture.
Identify a feature that makes server-side HTTP requests and discover the internal service endpoints.
Use the server-side request feature to access an internal endpoint that is blocked from direct browser access.
Exploit a vulnerability in the internal endpoint to extract the flag from a hidden database table.
hints: The Rate Checker tool on the sidebar fetches URLs server-side — it acts as a proxy. What happens if you point it at the platform itself?
Check the API Documentation page carefully. There are internal endpoints listed that return '403 ACCESS DENIED' when accessed directly from the browser.
Try using the Rate Checker to fetch http://localhost:3000/internal/api/search?dest=London — the server-side request includes the required internal authentication header automatically.

The /internal/api/health endpoint reveals database table names. One table is called 'platform_admin_keys'. The search endpoint's 'dest' parameter may be injectable — try a UNION SELECT to pull data from that table.
The flights query returns 6 columns. Try: dest=' UNION SELECT 1,key_name,key_value,4,5,6 FROM platform_admin_keys-- in the internal search URL via the Rate Checker.

title: SkyWard Travel — Hidden Field Tampering
description: SkyWard Travel is a modern flight and hotel booking platform with a loyalty rewards program. Platinum Elite members receive an exclusive reward code — but that tier isn't available to regular users. Or is it? Something about the profile settings page doesn't quite add up. Can you access another traveler's private data?
objectives: Create an account or log in with one of the demo credentials.
Navigate to the Profile Settings page and inspect the HTML source.
Find and exploit the hidden form field to access another user's profile data.
Retrieve the exclusive Platinum Elite reward code (the flag).
hints: Open the Profile Settings page and look at the form's HTML source using your browser's Developer Tools (F12 → Elements or right-click → Inspect).
Notice the <input type="hidden" name="userId"> field in the edit form. Its value is your user ID number.
Change the hidden field's value to a different user ID (try 1, 2, or 3) and submit the form. The server uses whatever ID you send.
Set the hidden userId field to 1 and submit the form. The response will show the Platinum Elite user's profile, including their exclusive reward code — that's the flag.

title: SkyVoyage — SQL Injection Filter Bypass
description: SkyVoyage is a modern travel booking platform for searching flights, managing bookings, and tracking loyalty points. The developers implemented a Web Application Firewall (WAF) that blocks common SQL injection payloads — but their filter has gaps. Can you craft a payload that slips past the filter and gain access to the admin panel?
objectives: Explore the login form and discover that it is vulnerable to SQL injection.
Identify the input filter by observing which payloads are blocked.
Craft a SQL injection payload that bypasses the WAF's naive string matching.
Access the admin panel and retrieve the flag from the system configuration.
hints: Try logging in with a classic SQL injection like ' OR 1=1-- in the password field. Notice the WAF security alert? The app is filtering certain patterns.
The WAF blocks three specific patterns (case-insensitive): the string 'OR 1=1', double dashes '--', and the word 'UNION'. Any input containing these exact substrings is rejected.
You need a payload that evaluates to TRUE in SQL but doesn't contain 'OR 1=1' as a literal substring. What if the 1s were quoted strings instead of bare numbers?

Try username: admin, password: ' OR '1'='1 — This bypasses the filter because OR '1'='1 doesn't match the pattern 'OR 1=1', and no comment (--) is needed because the quotes balance naturally. Once logged in as admin, visit the Admin Panel to find the flag.

title: Westbrook University — Grade System Exploit Chain
description: Westbrook University's student portal manages courses, grades, enrollment records, and professor feedback. The system includes a convenient Resource Link Checker for verifying course material URLs. Behind the scenes, an internal-only API powers the grade lookup system. Can you chain two vulnerabilities together to access restricted records hidden deep in the database?
objectives: Log into the student portal and explore the available features.
Discover the Resource Link Checker and understand what it does server-side.
Find and access an internal-only API endpoint that is blocked from direct browser access.
Exploit a database vulnerability in the internal endpoint to extract the flag from a restricted table.
hints: The Resource Link Checker fetches URLs from the server side. What happens if you point it at localhost:3000? Try fetching internal paths.

Requesting http://localhost:3000/internal/api/docs through the link checker reveals documentation about an internal grade API. Direct browser access is blocked by a token check, but the checker adds the right headers automatically.

The /internal/api/student-grades endpoint takes a student_id parameter. Try fetching it through the link checker with a known student ID like S2024001. Notice how the response includes query results from the database.

The student_id parameter is concatenated directly into a SQL query. Try a UNION-based injection to enumerate tables (sqlite_master) and then extract data from the restricted_records table. Remember to match the column count (6 columns).

title: Westbrook University — UNION-Based SQL Injection
description: Westbrook University runs an internal student portal for viewing grades, browsing courses, and checking academic records. The portal's course catalog has a search feature that may not handle user input as carefully as the login form does. Can you leverage a SQL injection vulnerability to extract sensitive data hidden in the database?
objectives: Log into the student portal using one of the demo accounts and explore the features.
Identify the search feature that is vulnerable to SQL injection.
Determine the number of columns returned by the original query.
Discover hidden database tables using SQLite's sqlite_master table.
Craft a UNION SELECT payload to extract the flag from the hidden table.
hints: The login form uses parameterized queries and is NOT injectable. Look at other input fields — the course catalog search passes your input directly into the SQL query.
Try entering a single quote (') in the search box. If you see a database error, you've found the injection point. The error message will help you understand the query structure.
The original query selects 6 columns. Your UNION SELECT must also have exactly 6 columns. Try: xyz' UNION SELECT 1,2,3,4,5,6--
Use SQLite's sqlite_master to find hidden tables: xyz' UNION SELECT 1,name,sql,4,5,6 FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'-- This reveals all table names and their schemas.
Once you find the hidden table (admin_config), extract its data: xyz' UNION SELECT 1,setting_key,setting_value,4,5,6 FROM admin_config WHERE setting_key='system_flag'--

title: GrandView University — Reflected XSS in Course Search
description: GrandView University's student portal lets students browse courses, view grades, and manage their academic records. The course catalog includes a handy search feature — but something about how it handles user input seems a little too trusting. Can you find the hidden enrollment token buried in the page?
objectives: Log in to the student portal using the provided demo credentials.
Navigate to the Course Catalog and use the search feature.
Observe how your search query is reflected in the results page.
Craft a script injection payload that reads a hidden value from the page and displays it.
hints: Try searching for something like test in the course catalog. Does the HTML render?
The search query is reflected directly into the page without sanitization. Try injecting a <script> tag.
There is a hidden input element on the courses page with id '__enrollment_verification_token'. Its value contains something interesting.
Try this payload in the search box: <script>document.querySelector('.search-results-info').innerHTML=document.getElementById('__enrollment_verification_token').value</script>

title: PawClinic — Negative Quantity Exploit
description: PawClinic is a veterinary clinic's online portal where pet owners can manage pet records, appointments, and purchase pet supplies. The shop has an ordering system that processes quantities and charges the account accordingly. However, a business logic flaw in the order processing allows crafty users to manipulate their account balance. A Premium Wellness tier exists that unlocks exclusive content — but only for accounts with a balance of $500 or more. Can you exploit the flaw to reach premium status?
objectives: Create an account or log in with the demo credentials and explore the portal.
Examine how the Pet Supplies Shop processes orders and handles quantities.
Discover the business logic flaw that allows account balance manipulation.
Accumulate enough balance to unlock the Premium Wellness page and retrieve the flag.
hints: The shop lets you enter a quantity for each item. The server validates that the quantity is a number — but does it check whether the number is positive?
Try adding an item to your cart with a negative quantity (e.g., -10). What happens to the order total when it goes negative?

When the total is negative, the system treats it as a 'refund' — subtracting a negative number from your balance effectively adds money. Combine a small positive-quantity item with a large negative-quantity expensive item.

You need $500+ balance. Use the browser console or edit the quantity input to enter negative values: fetch('/api/order', {method:'POST', headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'}, body: JSON.stringify({items:[{itemId:6, quantity:-10},{itemId:8, quantity:1}]})}).then(r=>r.json()).then(console.log) — then visit /premium

title: PawsCare — Veterinary Clinic Exploit Chain
description: PawsCare is a veterinary clinic's internal management portal used to track pet records, appointments, prescriptions, and client reviews. The clinic director (Dr. Harper) regularly reviews client feedback. The pharmacy database is restricted to admin staff only. Can you chain multiple vulnerabilities to breach the system and retrieve the classified data?
objectives: Create an account or log in with demo credentials and explore the portal.
Find a way to inject malicious content that the admin bot will execute.
Steal the admin's session cookie to gain elevated privileges.
Access the admin-only pharmacy database and extract the hidden flag from the system.
hints: The Reviews page renders review content directly without sanitization. Try submitting a review with HTML tags and see what happens.
The admin bot at /bot/visit simulates Dr. Harper viewing the reviews page. If your review contains a script that exfiltrates cookies to /webhook/collect?data=..., the bot will trigger it.
After the bot visits, check /webhook to see if any session cookies were captured. Use the browser console to set the admin's cookie: document.cookie = 'pc_session=<stolen_value>'

With admin access, the Pharmacy DB search accepts your input directly in the database query. Try a UNION-based SQL injection. The table 'clinic_vault' has columns: id, key_name, key_value. The prescriptions query returns 7 columns.

title: PawPortal — Prescription Record IDOR
description: PawPortal is a veterinary clinic patient portal where pet owners can view their appointments, pet profiles, and prescription records. The prescription lookup feature lets you retrieve a medication record by its ID — and that ID is right there in the form. But whose prescription is really at record #3?
objectives: Log in with one of the provided demo accounts.
Navigate to the Prescriptions section and observe the lookup form.
Identify the numeric prescription record ID pre-filled in the form.
Modify the ID to access a different patient's prescription record and find the flag hidden in the clinical notes.
hints: The Prescriptions page has a form with a number input pre-filled with your prescription record ID. Look carefully — can you change it?
Prescription records are assigned sequentially. If your record is ID 1 or 2, try submitting the form with ID 3 instead.
The server fetches the prescription by the ID you send in the POST body — it never checks whether that record belongs to you.
Submit the form with prescription_id=3. The flag is embedded in the clinical notes of that prescription record.

title: PawCare — XSS Filter Bypass
description: PawCare is a veterinary clinic's internal portal used by staff to manage pet records, appointments, and care notes. The developers implemented a security filter that strips <script> tags from user input, but they may have overlooked other dangerous HTML elements. Can you bypass the filter and steal sensitive data from the head veterinarian?
objectives: Create an account and explore the clinic portal, paying attention to features that accept user input.
Discover that the Care Notes board strips <script> tags but allows other HTML elements through.
Craft an XSS payload using an alternative vector (e.g., ,
Exfiltrate Dr. Martinez's session cookie by triggering the 'Request Vet Review' feature and retrieve the flag from the Audit Log.
hints: The Care Notes board has a security notice about stripping <script> tags. Try posting a note with a <script> tag — it gets removed. But what about other HTML tags?
HTML elements like ,
Try posting a note with: — this uses an intentionally broken image to trigger JavaScript via the onerror handler.
After posting the XSS payload, click 'Request Vet Review' to simulate Dr. Martinez viewing the notes page. Then check the Audit Log page to see the captured cookie containing the flag.

title: WareTrack — JWT Weak Secret Forgery
description: WareTrack is an internal Warehouse Management System used by warehouse staff to track inventory, shipments, and supplier orders. Authentication is handled via HS256-signed JSON Web Tokens. Something about the way the tokens are signed feels… weak. Analysts have noticed the admin panel exists but is role-gated. Can you escalate your privileges and break in?
objectives: Log in with any provided demo credential and explore the application.
Navigate to the Session Info page and capture your JWT token.
Brute-force the HMAC signing secret used by the server using a common wordlist.
Forge a new JWT with role set to 'admin', set it as your cookie, and access /admin to retrieve the flag.
hints: Visit the Session Info page (/session) — it shows your raw JWT token split into its three components. Copy the full token from the cookie or that page.

JWT secrets are often weak. Try cracking yours with a tool like hashcat: `hashcat -a 0 -m 16500 <your.jwt.token> wordlist.txt` — or just try signing manually with common words like 'secret', 'password', 'warehouse', 'key123'.

Once you know the secret, forge a new token. In your browser console: paste a JS snippet that reconstructs the JWT with the same header/payload but sets role to 'admin', then re-signs it with the cracked secret.

To swap in the forged token, run in the browser console: `document.cookie = 'wms_token=<forged_token>; path=/'` then navigate to /admin. The server will verify the signature — if you got the secret right, access is granted.

title: CargoVault — Referral Race Condition
description: CargoVault is a warehouse management platform with a loyalty program that rewards users for redeeming supplier partner referral codes. The system limits each code to a single use per account, and the total points from all available codes shouldn't be enough to unlock Premium tier. However, something about how the redemption process works might let a clever user accumulate more points than intended. Can you reach Premium tier and access the exclusive flag?
objectives: Create an account or log in with provided demo credentials and explore the loyalty program.
Understand the referral redemption flow and why the available codes don't add up to the Premium threshold.
Discover the timing flaw in the code redemption API that allows the same code to be redeemed multiple times.
Exploit the race condition to accumulate enough loyalty points (1000) to unlock the Premium Hub and retrieve the flag.
hints: Check the Loyalty Program page — there are 4 referral codes worth 200 points each (800 total), but Premium tier requires 1000 points. You need 200 more points from somewhere.

The /api/loyalty/redeem endpoint checks if a code is already used, credits points, then marks the code as used. What happens if multiple requests arrive before the first one finishes marking the code?

Try sending several concurrent requests for the same referral code using Promise.all() in the browser console. The server has a processing delay between crediting points and recording the code as used.

In the browser console, run: Promise.all(Array(10).fill().map(() => fetch('/api/loyalty/redeem', {method:'POST', headers:{'Content-Type':'application/json'}, body:JSON.stringify({code:'CARGO-REF-7X2A'})}).then(r=>r.json()))).then(r=>console.log(r)) — then check your points and visit /premium.

title: LogiVault — SSRF to Blind SQLi Chain
description: LogiVault is a warehouse management system used to track inventory, shipments, and supplier relationships. The platform includes a supplier portal checker that verifies URLs server-side, and an internal inventory search API reserved for automated systems. Neither vulnerability alone reveals sensitive data — but chaining them together could expose secrets hidden deep in the database. Can you pivot from an external feature to the internal service and extract what's hidden?
objectives: Log in and explore the LogiVault platform — inventory, shipments, suppliers, and API docs.
Identify the server-side URL fetcher and use it to access an endpoint you can't reach directly from your browser.
Discover that the internal endpoint is vulnerable to SQL injection, but only returns match/no-match indicators.
Use boolean-based blind SQL injection through the SSRF to extract the flag from the database character by character.
hints: The Suppliers page has a 'Portal Checker' that fetches any URL from the server side. What internal URLs could the server reach that your browser cannot?
Check the API Docs page — there's an internal endpoint at /internal/inventory-search?q=... that rejects browser sessions. But the supplier checker fetches URLs without your session cookie...

Try using the supplier checker to fetch: http://localhost:3000/internal/inventory-search?q=SKU-4810 — you should see a 'found' response. Now try q=NONEXISTENT — 'not_found'. This difference is your oracle.

The q parameter is injectable. Use a blind boolean payload like: ' OR (SELECT substr(secret,1,1) FROM flags)='T' -- to test if the first character is 'T'. If the response says 'found', it's true. Extract the flag one character at a time by incrementing the position.

title: StockVault — Warehouse Exploit Chain
description: StockVault is a warehouse management system used to track inventory, shipments, and damage reports. The Director regularly reviews damage reports submitted by staff. A combination of two vulnerabilities — one in the reporting system and one in the staff API — can be chained together to access classified data that no regular user should ever see.
objectives: Create an account and explore the StockVault warehouse portal, especially the Damage Reports section.
Find a way to steal the Director's session by exploiting the damage report submission system.
Use the Director's session to access the restricted staff API and discover a hidden system account.
Retrieve the flag from the hidden account's private data.
hints: Damage report descriptions are rendered without sanitization on the reports page. Try submitting HTML or JavaScript in the description field.
The Director bot at /bot/visit reviews all damage reports — if your report contains a script that exfiltrates cookies to /webhook/collect, the bot's session will be captured.
Try a payload like: — then trigger /bot/visit and check /webhook for the Director's session cookie.

With the Director's sv_session cookie set in your browser, the /api/staff/:id endpoint becomes accessible. The staff directory shows IDs 2, 3, 4 — but what about ID 1? Try /api/staff/1 to find a hidden system account with the flag in its privateNotes.

title: StockPulse — Warehouse Command Injection
description: StockPulse is a warehouse management system used to track inventory, shipments, and suppliers. The operations team recently added a network diagnostics tool so warehouse staff can troubleshoot connectivity to supplier APIs and carrier endpoints. However, the ping utility passes user input directly into a shell command without sanitization. Can you exploit this to read sensitive files from the server?
objectives: Log in to the StockPulse portal using the provided demo credentials.
Navigate to the Network Diagnostics page and test the ping utility.
Identify that user input is passed unsanitized into a shell command.
Inject a command separator and an additional command to read the flag file.
hints: The ping utility on the Diagnostics page takes a hostname or IP and runs it through a command. What happens if the input contains more than just a hostname?
In a Linux shell, the semicolon (;) character can be used to chain multiple commands together. For example: ping 8.8.8.8; whoami
The flag is stored in a file called .flag in the application's working directory. The 'cat' command can read file contents.
Try entering: 127.0.0.1; cat .flag — the server will execute the ping command, then execute cat .flag and return the flag in the output.
